start.text:
The Ascent campus announces itself the way all dead giants do: by fence.

Kilometres of it, doubled and tripled up the moor, sagging now, gates ajar — and then the campus proper rising out of the grey grass, a city that never had civilians: accommodation blocks with their windows blinded, the cooling towers Brother Casse drew aging year by year, the low hard buildings of the interface division half-sunk into the hill like teeth into a gum. No shine anywhere. That is the first strangeness, and Osy names it within a minute: in four hundred kilometres of quilted country, the campus alone is echoless — no stuck mornings, no shining crews, not one kept hour. 'Either nothing here was worth keeping,' she says, glassing the dead towers, 'or something here doesn't permit keeping. The Order's Hours stop at the fence line. Look — the moor holds echoes right up to the wire, and then nothing, like a mown edge.'

B walks the last kilometre oddly: not alarmed, not working — attentive in a new register, ears making constant small adjustments, the way he stands when the household plays the travel board and he watches the pieces move without understanding the game but knowing, in his bones, that a game is present.

At the interface division's door — steel, institutional, propped with a fire bucket in the universal grammar of skeleton crews everywhere — a figure waits, forewarned by whatever wire the Continuum still keeps: a man past seventy in a boiler suit gone soft as flannel, white hair cropped like Voss's, leaning on a stick with a machinist's grip. He watches you come the whole last hundred metres, and it is not a stranger's watching; it is the watching of a man checking a face against a long-held negative, and when you reach the door his eyes are wet, and furious about it, and he says — to you, only to you, in a voice like a cable under load:

'Twenty-two years I ran the calibration lab upstairs, son. I signed your gate pass the first morning you ever walked through this door, and I signed the manifest the last night you drove east and didn't come back.' The stick trembles once, is stilled. 'Rhee. You won't remember me. That was the finding, at the end — that you wouldn't remember any of it. I filed an objection.' He pushes the door wide on darkness and old machine air. 'Come in and read it.'

choices.text: In, behind the household's held breath — to the calibration lab, the personnel file, and a man who has waited four years to be remembered by someone who can't.
In, at the household's pace — her at your shoulder, the dog at hers — into the old machine air, to read a four-year objection filed on your behalf.

calibration_lab.text:
The calibration lab is a long room under the hill, and Rhee lights it stage by stage on a hand-cranked ring main, and stage by stage it assembles around you like a memory belonging to someone else: benches you do not know with wear patterns you do; a corner locker with no name and a shape of removed tape where a name was; and down the room's spine, the instrument the whole division existed to feed — a horseshoe of consoles around a chair, the chair harnessed and padded and sensored like the cockpit of a vehicle designed to travel nowhere.

'Civil interface,' Rhee says, cranking, not looking at you, telling it to the machines because telling it to your face is beyond even a machinist's grip. 'The Loom could already run its little local rewinds — you've seen the storms; that's the same physics, feral. The Ascent goal was global. Retrocausality at planetary scale, clean, aimed, reversible. And the whole scheme had one civil-engineering problem at the bottom of it, one, and the problem had a name, and the name was drift.' He straightens, and puts his hand on the chair's rail the way men touch tombstones. 'When you edit the past, every mind downstream of the edit gets rewritten with it. Fine — that's the product, God help us. But then: rewritten against what? Reconciliation requires a reference. To change everything, something has to hold still. The mathematics demanded a fixed point — a mind the rewrite engine treats as ground truth and never touches, so all other minds can be expressed as differences from it. The literature called it the calibration standard. The corridor —' and here the old man's voice does the thing cables do just before they part — 'the corridor just called it the anchor.'

Behind you, Osy has gone tournament-still, and her eyes are not on the chair. They are on you, and they have been on you since the word reference, doing the arithmetic she was born doing, arriving — you can feel it land — three sentences ahead of the old man's telling, at the place the whole file has been converging since the vault: an anchor is a mind the machine agrees never to rewrite.

And the edits go around you like weather around a yard.

choices.text: Ask it plainly, in the room built for the question: 'Who was the standard, Rhee? Who did the mathematics hold still?'
[Signal 3] Read the lab before he narrates it — the benches, the tape-shadowed locker, the plots still pinned at the horseshoe's far end. Never let the curator choose your first document.

read_the_lab.text:
You walk the lab with your hands clasped behind you, museum-style, the way you once walked a vault, because the vault taught you the method and the method paid: never let the curator choose your first document.

The room curates itself anyway; you just refuse the order of service. The benches first. The wear on the third stool from the window matches the wear on your own desk chair in the tower, the same starboard list of a man who sits on his left hip when he reads — and the bench in front of it carries, in fading grease pencil, a column of figures whose sevens cannot decide about the cross-stroke. Then the horseshoe's far end, where the plots are still pinned: validation curves, years of them, browning at the edges, and you read them the way you read everything, axes first. Reference-candidate residuals, battery after battery — a synthetic mind checked against the species and failing, failing, failing, the curves all wearing the same signature sag of a model that describes everyone and coheres as no one. And then the last sheet in the row, pinned slightly askew, by a hand in a hurry or a state: REF-CANDIDATE (LIVE) — RESIDUALS, FULL BATTERY. The curve is flat. Flat across the whole instrument, flat the way water is level, flat the way nothing biological has any right to be against a statistical construction of the entire species — unless the construction and the subject are, within measurement error, the same object.

There is no name on the sheet. There is a subject code, and there is the plot's own grammar — the axis conventions, the interval notation, the boxed question mark against one outlier since resolved — and you have been reading that grammar since your second year of university, because you invented it.

The model would not hold because it was not anybody. The live subject's residuals were flat. And the man who built the median mind of the species plotted this sheet about someone, in this room, in your handwriting, and the chair behind you is harnessed for a sitter of exactly your measurements, and the cold walks up your spine with the patience of arithmetic completing itself.

Behind you, Rhee watches you read. 'Most visitors head for the chair,' he says quietly. 'You went for the plots.' A pause, weighed on a machinist's scale. 'He did too. Every morning. First thing, like checking the weather.'

item.name: The Flat Curve
item.description: REF-CANDIDATE (LIVE) — RESIDUALS, FULL BATTERY: one browning sheet, pinned askew, flat across the whole instrument. A synthetic median that cohered as no one, and one live subject it could not tell itself apart from. Plotted in your own conventions, boxed question mark and all.

choices.text: Say it to him straight, so the room can stop holding it: 'It was never synthetic at the end, was it. Who was the standard, Rhee?'
Hold the plots one minute longer — first thing, like checking the weather — and then ask him, quietly, the question the room is already answering: who was the standard?

the_standard.text:
Rhee looks at you for a long time, and then he goes to the nameless locker, and opens it with a key from around his neck, and inside is a personnel file two hands thick, and on the cover, under the Ascent crest and the words CALIBRATION DIVISION — CL-4 — SINGLE HOLDER, is your name.

'Nobody was meant to be it,' he says. 'That's the part history will get wrong if any of us live to write it. The standard was meant to be synthetic — a reference mind built as a model, a statistical construction: the median human. Your firm had the analytics contract. You had the build.' He lays the file on the bench and opens it, and there is your handwriting, years of it, impatient crossbars marching through calculations you cannot remember making. 'Eight years, son. You spent eight years constructing the average mind of the species — every bias, every bandwidth, every ordinary limit, weighted true. Beautiful work. Dull as ditchwater and beautiful, the way real engineering is. And it kept failing validation, and the reason it kept failing is the joke God tells statisticians: a model of the median isn't a mind. It wouldn't cohere. It could describe the fixed point but it couldn't be one — until the night, and I was on shift for it, you walked into my lab with your validation curves and said the sentence I've had four years to stop hearing, and haven't.' The old man recites it with his eyes shut, word-perfect, a scripture and an indictment: ''The model won't hold because it isn't anybody. But its residuals against one live subject in the pool are flat, Rhee. Flat across the whole battery.' And I said whose, and you put your own scan on my bench, and you looked — I want this remembered; I filed it in the objection and I'll say it to your forgotten face — you looked pleased, son. Vindicated. Eleven thousand volunteers in the reference pool, and the man who built the median discovered he was standing on it. The most average mind ever measured. You. It was never an insult in this building. It was the load-bearing wall of the sky — and you carried it out of here every night in your skull and drove home.'

In the silence, the ring main hums. Osy has not moved. And B — B crosses the old lab, past the horseshoe, past the chair, and lies down at your feet with his chin across your boots, the way he lies at seams, and the whole room watches him do it, and Rhee laughs once — a terrible, tender, breaking sound — and says: 'Aye. What the dog said. There's the standard. There's the still point of the turning world, gentlemen. It buys honey. It plays chess badly. It came all this way to be told what it is.'

choices.text: Sit down, before the legs decide for you, and make Rhee tell the rest in order: the delivery, the Halfway, and why a man who was the standard woke up eleven months ago in a tower with no memory of any of it.
Stay standing — some findings deserve to be taken on your feet — and ask Rhee for the rest in order: the delivery, the Halfway, the tower with no memory in it.

rhee_delivery.text:
Rhee tells it in order, machinist to the end, and the order is its own horror, because you can hear the invoice lines accumulating toward a total he does not have.

The October delivery: the interface rig itself — the chair's twin, the mobile unit, crated with its biological reference kernel, escorted east by the only man whose presence the kernel could be validated against in transit. 'Calibration standard, biological. Bureaucrat's poetry. You rode with your own baseline in a box, son, down to the mooring they'd poured for it under the yard. Eighteen months of night freight to build the socket; one October night to seat the plug.' Why there, why deep: 'Distance. The mathematics wanted the anchor point far from the engine. The Loom's core ran overhead —' a hand, tilted at the ceiling, at the sky, at the memory of a sun-scale machine — 'and the fixed point sat in bedrock, east, at the end of the longest lever they could pour. Your yard is the anchor's house, and it was built around your numbers.'

And there the ring main runs down — the crank's spring spent, the lamps browning to embers — and Rhee does not stop talking; he has told this part to the dark for four years, and the dark is where it lives. It is you who moves: across the lab, to a crank your hands find without being sent, winding the old man's light back up while he stands with one palm flat on the chair's rail, keeping the worst of it for a room that can see him tell it. 'The Halfway next,' he says, when the lamps have climbed back to honest yellow. 'Ask it however you can stand to. There's no order of service makes it smaller.'

choices.text: The day itself, plainly: 'Tell me the Halfway, Rhee. From inside this room. The record, and no mercy — for him or for me.'
Aim him at the machine, the way she would — payoffs first: 'When the run went wrong, the engine had a choice to make. Tell it from the choice outward.'

rhee_halfway.text: And the Halfway. Rhee was here, in this room, on the horseshoe. 'The global run needed the standard live in the loop — not the model; you; the residuals had settled that argument. You'd been commuting to the yard for months. Every few weeks, east, two days in the mooring, back — synchronization sits, they were called. On the day, you were seated. We initiated. And I will tell you what the record shows and no more, because past this line everything is grief pretending to be data: forty-one seconds in, the reconciliation front hit something it could not express as a difference from you, and the engine did what engines do when the reference disagrees with the world.' The old man's hands settle flat on the bench, and stop. 'It kept the reference. It kept you, son. It threw away the world's version and kept yours, and the mismatch tore the sky in half, and when the lights came back the Loom was gone quiet, the campus was echoless, every one of my nine hundred was fifty-one, and the mooring — sealed, live, drawing power, holding one heartbeat and the one true day around it. We could not raise it. We have never raised it. We buried the project, and then, four years on —' he looks up, and his eyes go from you to Osy and back, and the machinist finally sets down his tools — 'the ledgers started. The purchases. The Editor, signing in a wardkey that this division issued exactly one of, ever. Single holder. I have spent four years praying the seals had held and the man in my doorway is the answer: they didn't. Something walks out of that mooring wearing your keys, son. And the only mercy I can find in tonight is that it isn't wearing you — because you're here, and you don't remember, and the wardkey never leaves the holder. So I have one question back, and it's the same as yours. If the standard is standing in my lab —' the stick, trembling, stilled — 'who is standing in the mooring?'

choices.text: Put your two exhibits on his bench — the hut survey with its margin, and the October line — and let the household and the machinist merge their files into one.
Answer his question with your own first — trade like colleagues, not witnesses — and then lay the exhibits on the bench: the margin, and the October line.

merge_files.text:
The files merge on the calibration bench, under the ring main's hum, and merged, they say a thing none of the parts quite dared to.

Rhee reads the hut survey twice, and the margin four times, and his verdict arrives as an engineer's, which is to say as a dated observation: 'The survey's recent. Two years at most — that paper yellows fast and it hasn't. Son, whoever wrote this used your hand and your method after the Halfway, above ground, four hundred kilometres from this room, and told you not yet.' He sets it grain to grain with the personnel file's marginalia, the way Voss set the strip, and the finding is the finding. Same hand. Twenty years of drift, or tiredness. 'And the freight line is mine — I signed the outer manifest; there's my counter-initial. One crate. One escort.' He looks up. 'One holder.'

It is Osy who says it, from the end of the bench, where she has been assembling the position in silence: 'Then we stop saying "something" — the data doesn't support "something." Wardkeys don't leave holders. Hands don't leave bodies. The Editor is him, Rhee. Him, seated in that mooring since the Halfway, holding the one true day — and also him, standing in your lab with eleven months of memory and a dog. Both. And the machine in the middle is the only thing on earth with the mechanism to make that true, because making one mind's versions agree is literally its job description.' She lets it stand a moment, and the ring main hums, and then she does the thing you love her for and will die knowing you loved her for, which is that she keeps going, at height, into the dark, without dropping a stitch: 'So the operating hypothesis is this. The engine kept its reference and lost the world. Since then, something in that mooring — him, a version of him, the engine wearing him; the difference may not be measurable from outside, Voss said so — has been running the ledger. Spending cities. Purchasing one outcome, over and over, at accelerating cost.' Her hand finds B's head without her noticing, the treaty ratified again in the layer below acknowledgment. 'Me. Alive, in place, and — per the bridge — kept off that ridge at the wrong hour. The last piece I don't have is why, and I've stopped expecting to like it.'

Rhee looks at her for a long moment, and then at you, and the old man's face does something complicated and gentle and appalled, and he says, quietly: 'You built the reference model, son. Eight years. Tell me — and think before you answer, because I proofread that model and I already know: whose conversational patterns did you weight the dialogue battery on? Whose game trees did you use for strategic cognition? The file says the sources were anonymized.' The stick taps the personnel file, once, twice, gavel-soft. 'But you were never any good at anonymizing what you loved.'

choices.text: Open the model's source manifest in the file, because the household does not flinch at pages — and read out whose mind you baked into the standard, eleven years ago, without asking her.
Let Osy run the merge — order out of order is her mother tongue — and stand ready at the personnel file for the question you can feel the bench assembling.

model_sources.text:
You open the source manifest with hands that know the way, and there it is, in your own impatient shorthand, in the ingestion logs of the median mind: SUBJ-2209 — DIALOGUE CORPUS (PRIMARY) — STRATEGIC BATTERY (PRIMARY) — 71 SESSIONS — CONSENT: BLANKET/FIRM.

Seventy-one sessions. You cross-reference the dates because she is already crossing them beside you, faster: Thursday lunchtimes, eleven years of them. The chess league. The firm's blanket research consent, buried in everyone's contract, covering 'workplace instrumentation' — the analytics company that analyzed its own staff, and the modeler who, needing a gold standard for how a sharp mind talks and plans and plays, reached for the one across the corner table every Thursday of his working life.

'The median holds because the residuals were flat against me,' you say slowly, reading your own crime in your own hand. 'But the model's cognition battery — the part that wouldn't cohere until the end — I tuned its coherence against her. She was the counterweight. The standard is my averageness, load-tested against her edge. The mathematics —' and the sentence arrives at the place everything has been converging, the vault, the dowser, the ledger, the yard spending like a man in love, and you say it in the flat voice because there is no other voice left — 'the mathematics has two people in it. It always had two people in it. The fixed point is me. But the thing the fixed point was calibrated to keep coherent — the definition of a mind worth expressing differences against —'

'— is me,' Osy finishes, from very far away and eleven inches to your left. 'The engine's whole concept of a mind that must not be lost. Is me.' She stands at the bench with her sister's cold in her face and something else underneath it that has no name in the household's registry yet, and she looks down at seventy-one Thursdays in the ingestion log, and she says, with a precision that costs what precision costs: 'You put me in the sky, colleague. Eleven years ago, without asking, because I was the best mind you knew and you were never any good at anonymizing what you loved. And now the sky is broken, and it is spending cities to keep its definition of coherence alive and in place — and every catastrophe on my wall, every pin, every thread, Pin, colleague, Pin —' and there it is, the fault line, her voice going down to bedrock and stopping just above it — 'traces to a courtesy you did my chess game eleven years ago in a file I never knew existed.'

The ring main hums. B is pressed against her legs, all his weight, and Rhee has bowed his head over his stick like a man at a graveside, and the lab holds the three of you and the file and the enormous, patient, four-year-old fact of it.

'Say the rest,' she says, to you, not gently. 'You're seeing something. I can hear you seeing it. Treaty. Out loud.'

choices.text: Say the rest: the ledger isn't a monster's. It's a modeler's — an engine still running its founder's last objective function, optimizing what he taught it to treasure. The Editor may be exactly what she called it in the vault: not the machine. The machine's opponent — or its conscience.
Open it slowly, reading the sources column aloud in inventory voice — some pages are easier walked at protocol pace — and let the name arrive in its own row.

say_the_rest.text:
You say the rest standing, because some findings will not be delivered sitting down.

'The engine isn't in love, and it isn't insane. It's faithful. It is running the objective I gave it: hold the reference, preserve coherence, and coherence has a name and a bunk in a transit station. Every purchase in Voss's folio is the machine doing exactly what its founder taught it mattered — and the founder is in the mooring, or a version of him is, and here is what I can finally see from inside the file.' You lay your hand flat on the personnel folder, on eight years of your own hand. 'The edits have two grammars. Voss showed us: the machine's purchases — vast, exact, costed to the decimal. And the margins — the boxed question marks, the not yet, the survey cached above eye height for a man walking a route only I would walk. The machine doesn't doubt, Osy. Doubt is the one thing you cannot get out of an objective function. Something in that mooring is annotating the machine's game with my doubt, in my hand, and leaving me — me, out here, the copy with the dog and no memory — a trail. Breadcrumbs pointed away from the yard until I'd be ready, and coordinates for when I am.' You look up, and the lab looks back: the machinist, the counterweight, the dog. 'Rhee asked who's standing in the mooring. I think the answer is: the part of me that stayed in the chair. And I think it has spent four years fighting the engine from inside with marginalia — losing slowly, because it also loves what the engine loves, and cannot make itself stop the spending — and I think it is waiting for the only reinforcement that could ever reach that room.' The words arrive at their own conclusion the way rivers arrive: 'The rest of him. Me. That's the not yet, and per the calendar of a control loop closing on its setpoint — it's nearly now.'

Rhee raises his head. And Osy — Osy takes the seventy-one Thursdays in one long look, and the bench, and you, and does the thing that will still astonish you on the last day of whichever life you get: she nods, once, and reaches past eleven years and one broken sky, and turns the personnel file to face her, and uncaps her pen.

'Then we go to the yard,' she says. 'Eyes open, on our terms, as planned — with one amendment. This stopped being your file tonight, colleague.' She writes, at the foot of the founding document of the broken world, in the minute vandal-proof hand, the household's whole doctrine, and signs it: 'Two skulls. One file. — O.'

'Whatever's in the chair gets both of us.'

item.name: The Founder's File
item.description: CL-4, single holder. Eight years of the median mind in your own hand; seventy-one Thursdays in the ingestion log; her name in the sky without her consent. At its foot, newly inked: 'Two skulls. One file. — O.'

choices.text: One question left for the lab before rest: her drift. Rhee ran calibration for twenty-two years — put Osy on his instruments and get the number the Warden only felt.
Ask it as an engineering request — Rhee, your instruments, her drift, a number — because the Warden felt it, and this household does not run on feelings it can measure.

drift_measurement.text:
Rhee's instruments are old and hand-cranked and were built to measure exactly this, and he runs Osy through them with the gentleness of a man who has measured the doomed before and refuses to do it rudely.

The number, when it settles, is worse than the Warden's frown and better than your fear, and it comes with a shape. 'Reference-frame decoherence, early second stage,' Rhee says, reading the drum. 'The engine's collection pattern — madam, your unwitnessed hours are being reconciled. Absorbed into the ledger as paid-for time. At the current rate, second stage runs eighteen to thirty months before the mornings stop coming back at all.' He lets the drum spin down, and then he does the thing you have watched him do all night: he tells the whole truth in order. 'But the rate isn't constant. Look at the trace. Your decoherence dives — flattens to nearly nothing — in two conditions. Witnessed time. And —' the old man looks up, over his glasses, with four years of grief and one night's terrible amusement in his face — 'proximity to a fixed point. The dog helps; whatever that animal is below the weave, he's a kind of pilot light. But the standard, madam. The standard is the standard.' He taps the flattest stretch of the trace, and reads out the annotation his own instrument printed under it, timestamped this evening, in the lab where the sky was built: 'Subject stability optimal at range: under two metres from reference.'

The lab is quiet. B's tail thumps once against the console housing, entirely satisfied with the finding.

And Osy — who walked out of a year of solitude with her method intact, who has never once needed anything she could not build, audit, or refuse; whose whole architecture, eleven years observed, is load paths that terminate in herself — Osy stands beside the drum in the ring main's hum reading the sentence that says her mind now holds together best within two metres of you, and her face goes through four things too fast to name, and lands on the fifth, which is the tournament stillness, and she says, in the flattest voice in her entire register:

'Well. That's inconvenient.'

And then, because she is Osy, and because the household does not flinch at pages, she picks up the trace, folds it with two exact creases, files it in the notebook against her sternum, next to the queen's cord — and moves her bedroll, that night, wordlessly, with terrible dignity, from the far wall of the lab to the space beside yours. Two metres. Under.

B lies down in the gap between, closing the circuit, and sighs the sigh of a dog whose work is, at last, officially recognized.

choices.text: Morning, and the audit — because the yard is next, the grace period is spent, and what this household is about to attempt has never been attempted by anyone, including the man who invented it.
Lie awake a while with the number and the two-metre arithmetic — then morning, and the audit, and the thing nobody, including its inventor, has ever attempted.

waypoint_three.text:
The audit convenes at dawn in the calibration lab, at the horseshoe, because Rhee insists — 'you'll want the good chairs; strategy deserves upholstery' — and the household takes stock in the place where the stakes were designed.

On the bench, the file, entire: a founder with eight missing years and a wardkey he cannot remember holding; a counterweight woven into the sky without consent, decohering, stable under two metres from the man who did it; a mooring four hundred kilometres east holding the one true day and something that annotates its own machine in doubt; a ledger accelerating toward a setpoint; a Reclaimer order three days behind a slow-filed report; and a dog, asleep on the founder's boots, keeping the whole arithmetic warm.

Rhee, who has appointed himself quartermaster on the grounds that 'my objection is four years old and finally has hands,' lays out what the campus can still give: the archive stacks below — 'your division's deep records; the mooring's as-builts; the interface protocols; everything the engine's founder might need to walk back into his own design' — and the hard question of the road home, with the order's lines walked by people who report faster than they think.

'But first,' the old man says, and sets on the bench, with ceremony, a machinist's toolroll and a whetstone, his metaphor and his gift, 'the oldest rule of this lab. Before the deep stacks, before the yard, before whatever is sitting in that chair wearing your keys, son — calibration. You do not take an instrument east without truing it.' He looks around the horseshoe, at the three of you, at the household the negative in his memory never predicted, and the machinist's grip eases on the stick, and for the first time all night the old man smiles like the morning he signed a gate pass for a young man with beautiful dull work ahead of him.

'So. The bench is yours. What does this household need you to be?'
